---
title: "FAQ"
---

## Mu-referencing {#mu-referencing}

**Do I need to write explicit MU-referencing like in NONMEM or nlmixr2?**

**No.** You do not need to write an explicit `MU_i` intermediate variable.
ferx detects the same structure automatically from the right-hand side of each
`[individual_parameters]` line.

In NONMEM and nlmixr2, MU-referencing is required in SAEM because the
conjugate-Gibbs E-step is only valid when the ETA→THETA relationship is
strictly linear on a transformed scale. In ferx, you simply write the
individual-parameter expression naturally:

```
CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)              # detected: multiplicative / log-normal
CL = exp(log(TVCL) + ETA_CL)         # detected: canonical MU form
CL = TVCL * (WT/70)^0.75 * exp(ETA_CL)  # detected: with covariate
CL = TVCL + ETA_CL                   # detected: additive
```

The parser records the ETA→THETA mapping and re-centres the inner-loop ETA
search at each outer iteration.

Patterns **not** auto-detected (falls back to `eta = 0` start):

- Two or more free thetas in the product (`TVCL * TVSCALE * exp(ETA_CL)`) — ambiguous anchor
- Compound eta expressions inside exp (`exp(ETA_CL + ETA_OCC)`)

Mu-referencing affects convergence speed and robustness, not the final MLE.
Disable with `mu_referencing = false` in `[fit_options]` to reproduce
pre-automatic behaviour.

---

## Conditional logic {#conditional-logic}

**Can I use `if` / `else` statements like in NONMEM or nlmixr2?**

**Yes.** Two forms are supported in `[individual_parameters]` and `[odes]`:

```
# Block form
if (WT > 70) {
  CL = TVCL * (WT / 70)^0.75 * exp(ETA_CL)
} else if (SEX == 1) {
  CL = TVCL * 1.2 * exp(ETA_CL)
} else {
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
}

# Inline (ternary) form
CL = if (SEX == 1) TVCL * 1.5 else TVCL
```

Compared to NONMEM: C-style `{braces}` and operators (`>`, `==`, `&&`) rather
than Fortran-style `IF THEN … ENDIF` and `.GT.`, `.LE.`, etc.

Compared to nlmixr2: near-verbatim match — replace `<-` with `=`.

**Caveat:** `==` and `!=` do bitwise float comparison. They work for
integer-coded covariates (`SEX == 1`) but are unreliable for continuous
thresholds. Prefer `WT >= 70 && WT < 80` over `WT == 75`.

**Caveat:** when a parameter is assigned inside an `if` block, mu-reference
detection is skipped for that parameter. See
[Individual Parameters](model-dsl/individual-parameters.qmd#interaction-with-mu-referencing).

---

## Optimizer selection {#optimizer}

**Which outer optimizer should I pick?**

`slsqp` (the default) is the right choice for most models — it is fast, handles
box constraints cleanly, and behaves well on the log-transformed parameter scale.

Reach for a different optimizer when SLSQP misbehaves:

- **`bobyqa`** — derivative-free, robust when FOCE's FD gradients are noisy
  and SLSQP stalls or oscillates. Slower per iteration on smooth problems.
- **`trust_region`** — second-order Newton trust-region. Can be faster near
  convergence; tune the CG budget with `steihaug_max_iters` (default 50) for
  models with more than ~50 packed parameters.
- **`lbfgs` / `bfgs`** — fall back to these only when NLopt is unavailable.

See [Fit Options — Optimizer choices](model-dsl/fit-options.qmd#optimizer-choices).

---

## Block omega {#block-omega}

**How do I switch from diagonal to block omega?**

Each `omega` variance becomes a diagonal entry; off-diagonals start at `0.0`:

```
# Before
omega ETA_CL ~ 0.1
omega ETA_V1 ~ 0.1
omega ETA_Q  ~ 0.1

# After (3×3 block, 6 values: 1+2+3)
block_omega (ETA_CL, ETA_V1, ETA_Q) = [
  0.1,
  0.0, 0.1,
  0.0, 0.0, 0.1
]
```

Block omega regularises poorly-identified parameters by letting them share
variability. Starting all off-diagonals at `0.0` lets the fit recover the
correlations from the data. See [Parameters — Block omega](model-dsl/parameters.qmd#block-omega-correlated-random-effects).

---

## Sigma scale {#sigma-scale}

**Is sigma estimated on the SD scale or the variance scale?**

**SD scale.** `sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.1` for a proportional model is a **10% CV**
initial value.

The fit YAML emits both `estimate` (SD) and `variance` (SD²). For proportional
components it also emits `cv_pct = estimate * 100`. See
[Error Model — Sigma scale](model-dsl/error-model.qmd#sigma-scale).

---

## FOCEI vs FOCE {#focei-vs-foce}

**When should I use FOCEI instead of FOCE?**

Use FOCEI (default) whenever the error model is proportional or combined. FOCE
evaluates the residual variance at η = 0 (population mean); FOCEI evaluates it
at the individual EBE η̂, capturing the dependence between residual error and
individual predictions.

For a purely additive error model, FOCE and FOCEI are equivalent (the
interaction term is zero). Using FOCE for additive-error models can save
a small amount of computation.

---

## ODE vs analytical {#ode-vs-analytical}

**When must I use an ODE model?**

Use an ODE model when the kinetics cannot be expressed as a sum of exponentials:

- Saturable (Michaelis-Menten) elimination
- Target-mediated drug disposition (TMDD)
- Transit compartment absorption
- Custom multi-compartment structures

For standard first-order kinetics use the analytical models (`one_cpt_oral`,
`two_cpt_oral`, etc.) — they are 10–100× faster and support Enzyme autodiff
gradients. See [ODE Models](model-dsl/odes.qmd#when-to-use-ode-vs-analytical).

---

## Porting from NONMEM {#porting-nonmem}

**How do I port a NONMEM model to ferx?**

1. Drop all `$ABBR`, `$MU_i`, `$SIZE` boilerplate — not needed in ferx.
2. Translate `$PK` assignments to `[individual_parameters]` expressions with
   `=` instead of `=`.
3. Replace `THETA(i)`, `ETA(i)`, `EPS(i)` with descriptive names
   (`TVCL`, `ETA_CL`, `PROP_ERR`).
4. If the NONMEM model uses explicit `MU_i = LOG(THETA(i))` lines, drop them —
   ferx detects the mu-reference automatically.
5. Map `$SUBROUTINE ADVAN` to the equivalent ferx `pk` function:
   - ADVAN1 → `one_cpt_iv_bolus`
   - ADVAN2 → `one_cpt_oral`
   - ADVAN3 → `two_cpt_iv_bolus`
   - ADVAN4 → `two_cpt_oral`
   - ADVAN11 → `three_cpt_iv_bolus`
   - ADVAN12 → `three_cpt_oral`
6. Map `$ERROR` to `[error_model]`. Remember sigma is on the **SD scale** in
   ferx (not variance).

---

## Gradient methods {#gradient-methods}

**When does Enzyme autodiff kick in, and when does ferx fall back to finite differences?**

The gradient path is resolved per-subject at runtime:

| Path | When used |
|---|---|
| `AdSingleSnapshot` (Enzyme reverse-mode) | Subject has no time-varying covariates; analytical PK model |
| `AdEventDriven` (Enzyme reverse-mode, event-driven) | Subject has TV covariates; 1- or 2-cpt analytical model |
| `Fd` (central finite differences) | `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` build, ODE model, or TV-cov on 3-cpt model |

`fit$gradient_used` (in R) shows which path was resolved. See
[Autodiff](ferx-core/autodiff.qmd) for details.
